saida_bruta:
There was nothing gigantic about the appearance of the man starting to stir on the sleeping-porch of a Dutch Colonial home in Zenith’s Floral Heights residential area.

His name was George F. Babbitt. Now forty-six in April 1920, he produced nothing specific, neither butter nor shoes nor verse, yet he proved deft at the trade of selling houses for sums exceeding what buyers could manage.

His big head was pink, his brown hair sparse and dry. In sleep his face looked infantile, in spite of wrinkles and the red marks from spectacles on his nose. He was not obese but thoroughly well nourished; his cheeks formed pads, and the smooth hand resting limply on the khaki blanket seemed a bit swollen. He looked successful, thoroughly wedded and prosaic; equally prosaic was this sleeping-porch overlooking one large elm, two tidy lawns, a concrete drive, and a corrugated iron garage. Still Babbitt once more dreamed of the fairy child, a vision more romantic than scarlet pagodas beside a silver sea.

saida_bruta:
There was nothing enormous in the look of the man beginning to wake on the sleeping-porch of a Dutch Colonial house in Zenith’s Floral Heights neighborhood.

His name was George F. Babbitt. Now forty-six in April 1920, he made nothing particular, neither butter nor shoes nor poetry, yet he showed skill in the business of selling houses for prices beyond what purchasers could afford.

His large head was pink, his brown hair thin and dry. In slumber his face appeared babyish, despite wrinkles and the red dents from glasses on his nose. He was not fat but fully well fed; his cheeks made cushions, and the smooth hand lying slackly on the khaki blanket appeared slightly puffy. He seemed prosperous, completely married and ordinary; just as ordinary was this sleeping-porch viewing one big elm, two neat lawns, a concrete driveway, and a corrugated iron garage. Still Babbitt again dreamed of the fairy child, an image more romantic than scarlet pagodas by a silver sea.
